title: England makes anal intercourse a capital crime with the Buggery Act
description: Under King Henry VIII, the English Parliament passed the Buggery Act 1533, the country's first secular law against anal intercourse, punishable by death. The statute became the foundation for centuries of anti-sodomy legislation across England and its colonies.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Cabeza de Vaca records same-sex marriages among Native Americans
description: Spanish explorer Alvar Nunez Cabeza de Vaca published an account of his 1528-1536 journey across North America that described same-sex marriages and men living in women's roles among a Native American tribe, an early European record of Indigenous gender diversity.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: England's buggery law is extended to Wales
description: Henry VIII gave royal assent to the Laws in Wales Act 1542, which brought Wales under English law and thereby extended the capital offense created by the Buggery Act 1533 to Welsh territory.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Mary I repeals Henry VIII's laws, including the Buggery Act
description: On taking the English throne, Mary I revoked the legislation enacted under Henry VIII during the Reformation of the 1530s. The repeal swept away the Buggery Act 1533, temporarily removing the secular death penalty for sodomy in England.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Elizabeth I restores the Buggery Act in England
description: Between 1558 and 1563, Elizabeth I reinstated the laws of Henry VIII that Mary I had repealed, among them the Buggery Act 1533. Sodomy again became a capital offense under English secular law.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Wojciech of Poznan is burned for 'crimes against nature' in Poland
description: Wojciech z Poznania, regarded publicly as a woman, had married Sebastian Slodownik in Poznan and later Wawrzyniec Wloszek in Krakow. In 1561 Wojciech was tried and burned at the stake for 'crimes against nature'.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: The Boxer Codex describes same-sex marriage in the pre-colonial Philippines
description: The Boxer Codex, a Spanish manuscript from around 1590, recorded that same-sex marriage was a normalized practice in the pre-colonial Philippines.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Hu Tianbao is executed in Fujian and later deified as Tu'er Shen
description: During the 17th century, Chinese authorities executed Hu Tianbao of Fujian province. Local people afterwards venerated him as Tu'er Shen, the rabbit god of love between men, and built a temple in his honor.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Sweden introduces its first secular law against male sodomy
description: King Charles IX introduced Sweden's first secular statute against male sodomy in 1608. The prescribed punishment was death.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Virginia Colony briefly makes male sodomy a capital offense
description: The Colony of Virginia enacted a military order in 1610 that criminalized male sodomy with the death penalty. The order lapsed later that year when martial law ended after control of the colony changed hands.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Brandenburg-Prussia makes sodomy punishable by death
description: In 1620 Brandenburg-Prussia criminalized sodomy and set death as the punishment.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Richard Cornish is hanged for sodomy in the Virginia Colony
description: Richard Cornish, a ship's captain in the Colony of Virginia, was tried for sodomy in 1624 and executed by hanging.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: First known prosecution for lesbian activity in North America
description: In March 1648 Sarah White Norman was charged in Plymouth, Massachusetts, with lewd behavior on a bed with Mary Vincent Hammon, the earliest known prosecution for lesbian activity in North America. Hammon, under sixteen at the time, was not prosecuted.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: First criminal trial for homosexuality on Canadian territory
description: A military drummer at the French garrison of Ville-Marie in New France, on the site of present-day Montreal, was sentenced to death by local Sulpician priests in 1648 in the first known criminal trial for homosexuality in what is now Canada. After Jesuits in Quebec City intervened, his life was spared on the condition that he serve as New France's first permanent executioner.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Connecticut Colony's sodomy law also punishes lesbian intercourse
description: In 1655 the Connecticut Colony passed a law against sodomy that, unusually for the period, also included punishment for lesbian intercourse.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Virginia Colony recriminalizes sodomy by adopting English common law
description: The Colony of Virginia adopted English common law in 1661, which again made sodomy between men a criminal offense in the colony.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Earliest Ethiopian literary reference to homosexuality between nuns
description: The hagiography The Life and Struggles of Our Mother Walatta Petros, completed in 1672, contains the first known reference to homosexuality between nuns in Ethiopian literature.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Denmark makes 'relations against nature' punishable by death
description: In 1683 the Kingdom of Denmark criminalized 'relations against nature' and prescribed the death penalty for the offense.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Kagemachaya, an early Japanese gay bar, opens
description: Between 1688 and 1704, during Japan's Genroku era, the Kagemachaya opened as an early Japanese establishment serving men seeking male company, described as the country's first gay bar.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Catharina Margaretha Linck is executed for female sodomy
description: Catharina Margaretha Linck, who had lived as a man and married a woman, was executed for female sodomy in Germany in 1721 — one of the last documented executions of a woman for this offense in Europe.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Police raid Mother Clap's molly house in London
description: In 1726 police raided Mother Clap's molly house, a London meeting place for men who had sex with men. The raid led to trials and the execution of three men.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: The Utrecht sodomy trials begin in the Netherlands
description: A wave of sodomy prosecutions that started in Utrecht in 1730 spread across the Dutch Republic, leading to the execution of more than a hundred men and the banishment of hundreds more.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Jens Andersson is tried in Norway after marrying as a man
description: Jens Andersson, assigned female at birth but living as a man, was imprisoned and put on trial in Norway in 1781 after marrying Anne Kristine Mortensdotter in a Lutheran church. Asked about his gender, Andersson answered that he believed he belonged to both.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Prince Kraison becomes the first openly queer member of the Chakri dynasty
description: Prince Kraison of Thailand, son of Rama I, was the first openly queer member of the Chakri dynasty and maintained loving relationships with the theatre actors Khun Thong and Yaem.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Jeremy Bentham argues for decriminalizing sodomy in England
description: Philosopher Jeremy Bentham wrote in 1785 that sodomy should no longer be a crime in England, making him among the first thinkers to argue for its decriminalization. His essay remained unpublished during his lifetime.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Death of Frederick the Great of Prussia
description: King Frederick the Great of Prussia, whose sexuality has been the subject of extensive historical debate and who is widely discussed as having loved men, died in 1786.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: France becomes the first West European country to decriminalize sodomy
description: The French Penal Code of 1791 omitted sodomy from its list of offenses, making France the first West European country to decriminalize homosexual acts between consenting adults. The code also applied in Andorra and in French colonies including Haiti.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Dream of the Red Chamber is published with an openly bisexual character
description: Cao Xueqin's novel Dream of the Red Chamber was published in China in 1791. The book features an openly bisexual character and an account of an attack on a man for his same-sex relations.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Monaco decriminalizes sodomy
description: In 1793 Monaco removed sodomy from its criminal law, following the decriminalization model of revolutionary France.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Prussia abolishes the death penalty for sodomy
description: The Kingdom of Prussia ended capital punishment for sodomy in 1794, replacing execution with lesser penalties while keeping the act itself criminal.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Luxembourg decriminalizes sodomy
description: Luxembourg removed sodomy from its criminal law in 1794, adopting the decriminalization introduced by the French Penal Code of 1791.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Belgium decriminalizes sodomy
description: In 1795 sodomy ceased to be a crime on the territory of present-day Belgium, which came under French law and its 1791 Penal Code after annexation by revolutionary France.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)
